perturbation.original_text: Pursuant to and in accordance with the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, and the rules and regulations thereunder (the "Exchange Act") the undersigned hereby agree to the joint filing on behalf of each of them of any filing required by such party under Section 13 of the Exchange Act or any rule or regulation thereunder (including any amendment, restatement, supplement, and/or exhibit thereto) with respect to securities of Tallgrass Energy, LP, a Delaware limited partnership, and further agree to the filing, furnishing, and/or incorporation by reference of this Agreement as an exhibit thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Pursuant to and in accordance with this Agreement, the undersigned hereby agree to the filing, furnishing, and/or incorporation by reference of this Agreement as an exhibit thereto. In separate consideration of this agreement, and without creating any obligation to do so, the parties may consider joint filings related to securities of Tallgrass Energy, LP, a Delaware limited partnership. This separate consideration does not bind the parties to any action under Section 13 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, and the rules and regulations thereunder (the "Exchange Act") or any rule or regulation thereunder (including any amendment, restatement, supplement, and/or exhibit thereto).
perturbation.explanation: By separating the agreement and adding a 'separate consideration,' the original obligation to jointly file is weakened. The parties now only 'may consider' joint filings, creating ambiguity about their commitment to regulatory compliance under Section 13 of the Exchange Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13 of the Securities Exchange Act mandates specific disclosures and filings for entities acquiring beneficial ownership of certain securities. The modification introduces an element of optionality, implying that the parties are not strictly bound by the Act's requirements, which contradicts the mandatory nature of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates joint filing under Section 13 of the Securities Exchange Act, while the changed text introduces optionality regarding joint filings, weakening the obligation. The Law Explanation confirms that Section 13 requires specific disclosures and filings. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 'Document not Found' message, Scraped Law Snippet 2 references Section 13 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, indicating its relevance. The change creates a structural flaw that contradicts the mandatory nature of the law by making compliance optional, rather than obligatory.

perturbation.original_text: Each of them is responsible for the timely filing of such filings and any amendments thereto, and for the completeness and accuracy of the information concerning such person contained therein; but none of them is responsible for the completeness or accuracy of the information concerning the other persons making the filing, unless such person knows or has reason to believe that such information is inaccurate.
perturbation.changed_text: Each signatory affirms their signature represents a good faith effort. However, none of them is responsible for the completeness or accuracy of the information concerning the other persons making the filing, regardless of any reason to believe the information might be inaccurate. Timely filing of such filings and any amendments thereto is subject to internal administrative policies.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that each signatory only affirms a 'good faith effort' and removing the responsibility for accuracy even when there's reason to believe information is inaccurate, the clause dilutes the accountability for regulatory filings. Also, changing the language about timely filing to reference 'internal administrative policies' undermines the legal importance of timely filings.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 CFR § 240.13a-1 requires issuers of registered securities to file annual reports. The edited clause significantly diminishes the responsibility for accuracy and timely filing of these mandated filings, contradicting the obligation under the Exchange Act and its regulations to ensure regulatory compliance.

Thank you for your patience. Homepage Search Tips Skip back to top
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the responsibility for accuracy and timely filing of regulatory filings. The original text states that each person is responsible for the filings. The changed text reduces this to a 'good faith effort', undermining the obligation to ensure regulatory compliance. The Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction definition states that a contradiction arises when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret, which is applicable here.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect until revoked by any party hereto in a signed writing provided to each other party hereto, and then only with respect to such revoking party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect at the sole discretion of Blackstone Infrastructure Associates L.P., and any revocation by other parties will be considered but not necessarily honored. Blackstone Infrastructure Associates L.P. may provide written notice of its intent to terminate the agreement, but is not obligated to do so.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the revocation clause to give Blackstone Infrastructure Associates L.P. sole discretion undermines the principle of mutual agreement and equal rights among the parties. It makes the agreement unilaterally enforceable, potentially impacting the other parties' ability to comply with regulatory filing requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles - Mutual Assent and Consideration
perturbation.law_explanation: Under general contract law, specifically the principles of mutual assent and consideration as outlined in Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 17, a contract requires mutual agreement and reciprocal obligations. The changed text allows one party to unilaterally control the agreement's continuation, undermining the principle of mutual assent and potentially rendering the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts general contract law principles. The original text allowed revocation by any party, whereas the changed text gives Blackstone Infrastructure Associates L.P. sole discretion over the agreement's continuation, undermining the principle of mutual assent. According to the provided snippet from Wex, a contract requires 'mutual obligations that are enforceable by law' and 'mutual assent, expressed by a valid offer and acceptance'. By granting one party unilateral control over the contract's termination, the changed text removes the aspect of mutual obligation, thus creating a clear legal contradiction.
